content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
PO BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO 95814 • 100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814
[illegible] • Enforcement • Conflict of Interest
322-6441 322-6441

March 1, 1979

Michael L. White
3975 Madison
Riverside, CA 92504

Dear Mr. White:

In response to your letter of February 27, 1979, I am
providing you with the following informal advice pursuant
to Government Code Section 83114(b).

You have asked whether advertisements you will place
in the newspaper which you own and publish and in support
of your campaign are reportable. You state that the newspaper will not charge for the advertising. The Political
Reform Act's definition of "contribution includes, "the
candidate's own money or property used on behalf of his
candidacy." Government Code Section 82103. Therefore,
the donation of the advertising space is a contribution
to your campaign and should be reported as such by the
campaign pursuant to Government Code Section 84102(b).
That section requires, among other things, a listing of
the name of a contributor of $100 or more and the amount
of the contribution. However, the Fair Political
Practices Commission
has exempted regularly published newspapers from the
reporting of expenditures they make to support or oppose
candidates or measures. (Cal. Admin. Code Section
[illegible](c)(4)(A),
a copy of which is enclosed.) Therefore, even though the
campaign is required to report receipt of the advertising
space, the newspaper will incur no obligation to report the
expenditure for the advertising space.

You also ask how to value the advertising space for
purposes of reporting the contribution. Government Code
Section 81011 is applicable to reporting of contributions
of goods and services. That section states:

Whenever in this title the amount of goods,
services, facilities or anything of value
other than money is required to be reported,

3915 Madison
Riveraide, CA

February 27, 1991


Mr. Dan Lowenstein
Fair Political Practices Commission
1102 "K" Street
Sacramento, CA 95814


Dear Mr. Lowenstein:

This is a request for a compliance determination of the correct
method of disclosing donated newspaper advertising space.

As a publisher of a local newspaper, in which I am the sole
proprietor, I anticipate a donation of advertising space to be used
in that publication during an upcoming bid for a local city
council seat. I would appreciate the commission's advice on
how the use of the space needs to be reported. Should the
commission decide the space represents a financial contribution
to my campaign, would it be reported at the real value it costs
me as an owner to publish the ad or at the value of space sold to
regular advertisers?

Your prompt attention to this matter is appreciated. If any
further information is necessary, please contact me at
(714) 588-5390.

Sincerely,


Michael L. White

Michael L. Wm
March 8, 1979
Page Two

The project shall be "at a reduced rate,
board while at your facility to dis-
cussed, and if discretion. The models,
services, or facilities that be appended
to a report of this

Therefore, the entire project space would be the
price the model could command and the advertising space, not your usual contribution publish the
ads.

I hope this information has been helpful to you. If
you have any further questions, please do not hesitate to
call me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Leo C. Rosenthal
Director
Legal Division

LCR:pln
Enclosure

sections.question: You have asked whether advertisements you will place in the newspaper which you own and publish and in support of your campaign are reportable. You state that the newspaper will not charge for the advertising... You also ask how to value the advertising space for purposes of reporting the contribution.
sections.conclusion: Therefore, the donation of the advertising space is a contribution to your campaign and should be reported as such by the campaign pursuant to Government Code Section 84201(g)... the newspaper will incur no obligation to report the expenditure for the advertising space. Therefore, the value of the advertising space would be the price the newspaper would charge customers for similar advertising space, not your cost as an owner to publish the ads.
sections.question_synthetic: A newspaper publisher seeking city council office asked whether free advertising space he provides to his own campaign is reportable as a contribution, and if so, how to value it for reporting purposes.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The donated advertising space is a reportable campaign contribution that must be reported by the campaign at fair market value (the price charged to regular advertisers), though the newspaper itself has no reporting obligation under the exemption for regularly published newspapers.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Clear Q/C structure present. Document includes both the incoming request letter (dated Feb 27, 1978) and the FPPC response letter (dated March 8, 1979). Minor date discrepancy in letterhead (1978 vs 1979) appears to be typographical error in source document.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: You have asked whether advertisements you will place in the newspaper which you own and publish and in support of your campaign are reportable. You state that the newspaper will not charge for the advertising... You also ask how to value the advertising space for purposes of reporting the contribution.

CONCLUSION: Therefore, the donation of the advertising space is a contribution to your campaign and should be reported as such by the campaign pursuant to Government Code Section 84201(g)... the newspaper will incur no obligation to report the expenditure for the advertising space. Therefore, the value of the advertising space would be the price the newspaper would charge customers for similar advertising space, not your cost as an owner to publish the ads.

embedding.summary: FPPC Director Lee Rosenthal advises Michael White that free newspaper advertising he provides to his own city council campaign is a reportable contribution valued at fair market rates, not his production costs, though the newspaper has no separate reporting obligation.
